Active Work Architecture
─[ Skill and Agent Ownership Boundaries ]─ ┐

Skill and Agent Ownership Boundaries

Context

Today all skills live in .lore/skills/ and all agents in .lore/agents/ with no distinction between framework-owned and operator-owned content. This creates three problems:

  1. Browsing confusion — an operator sees 20+ items and can’t tell which are framework commands, which were auto-discovered, and which they added themselves
  2. Sync risk — sync-framework.sh overwrites built-in skills using a hardcoded list. If the list is wrong, operator content gets clobbered
  3. Script interference — generate-agents.sh derives agents from skill domains and could reorganize operator-defined domain assignments

The goal is a clear boundary where operators can manage their own skills and agents without worrying about Lore touching, regrouping, or overwriting them.

Three Ownership Tiers

Before choosing an approach, the ownership model itself needs defining. There are three distinct categories today, even though they’re not formalized:

TierExamplesCreated ByUpdated BySync Behavior
Framework commandslore-capture, lore-create-skill, lore-statusLore templatesync-framework.shOverwritten
Discovered gotchasbash-macos-compat, gitignored-nav-driftLore during sessionsLore or operator???
Operator-defined(hypothetical) deploy-staging, api-auth-flowOperator manuallyOperator onlyNever touched

The messy boundary is tier 2 — Lore creates these during sessions, assigns them domains, and could update them. But operators might customize them. Once created, who owns them?

Two models for tier 2:

  • Lore owns until operator edits — risky, hard to detect, creates implicit state
  • Operator owns immediately — Lore creates the file but never modifies it again. Simpler, safer.

Approaches Considered

Option 1: Underscore Prefix + Frontmatter

Approach: Prefix all Lore-owned skills/agents with _ (e.g., _lore-capture/). Add owner: lore or owner: operator to YAML frontmatter. Scripts filter on frontmatter; humans see the prefix.

Pros:

  • Visual sort order puts system skills at top in file browsers and terminals
  • Immediate browsing clarity — no need to open files
  • Frontmatter gives scripts a reliable filter independent of naming
  • Simple naming convention, no structural changes

Cons:

  • Underscore prefix is unusual and looks “internal/private”
  • Every existing instance needs migration (rename all built-in skills)
  • Discovered skills (tier 2) need a decision: _bash-macos-compat or bash-macos-compat?
  • Two parallel conventions (prefix + frontmatter) could drift

Effort: Medium — rename skills, update sync script lists, update generation scripts, migrate instances

Option 2: Separate Subdirectories

Approach: Split into .lore/skills/system/ and .lore/skills/custom/ (same for agents). System skills are synced/generated. Custom skills are never touched.

Pros:

  • True physical separation — impossible for scripts to accidentally cross the boundary
  • No naming conventions needed — names stay clean
  • Clear mental model: “system/ is Lore’s, custom/ is mine”

Cons:

  • Deeper paths (.lore/skills/system/lore-capture/SKILL.md)
  • Knowledge map tree gets noisier with extra directory level
  • Platform copies (.claude/skills/) would need matching structure or flattening
  • Routing: when Lore creates a discovered skill, which dir does it go in?

Effort: High — restructure directories, update all scripts, update platform sync, update docs

Option 3: Frontmatter Only

Approach: Add owner: lore or owner: operator to skill/agent frontmatter. No naming or structural changes. Scripts read frontmatter to decide what to touch.

Pros:

  • No migration needed for directory structure
  • Skill names stay clean and consistent
  • Minimal code changes — just add frontmatter filtering to generation scripts

Cons:

  • Not visible when browsing a directory listing — must open files to see ownership
  • Doesn’t solve the browsing clarity problem the operator raised
  • Relies on scripts always checking the field — easy to forget in new code

Effort: Low — add frontmatter field, update generation scripts to filter

Option 4: Naming Convention (No Underscore) + Frontmatter

Approach: All framework command skills keep the lore- prefix they already have (lore-capture, lore-create-skill). Non-lore- prefixed skills are operator-owned by convention. Frontmatter owner field makes it explicit for scripts. Discovered skills (tier 2) get created without the lore- prefix, making them operator-owned from birth.

Pros:

  • No migration needed — framework skills already start with lore-
  • Natural naming: lore-capture is clearly a framework command, bash-macos-compat is clearly a discovered skill
  • Frontmatter provides the authoritative filter for scripts
  • Tier 2 ownership is clean: Lore creates it, operator owns it immediately

Cons:

  • lore- prefix is a soft convention, not enforced (operator could name a skill lore-my-thing)
  • Some existing non-command skills have descriptive names that don’t follow this pattern (they’d all be operator-owned, which is correct)
  • Agents don’t have a lore- prefix convention today

Effort: Low-Medium — add frontmatter field, update generation scripts, define agent convention

Key Design Questions

  1. What happens to discovered skills on sync? If operator-owned from birth, sync never touches them. But what if Lore discovers the same gotcha again in a new session — should it update the existing skill or skip it?

  2. Agent derivation scope. Should generate-agents.sh only derive agents from Lore-owned skills, or from all skills? If an operator adds a skill with domain: Infrastructure, should Lore auto-create an infrastructure agent?

  3. Registry scope. Should skills-registry.md and agent-registry.md include both system and operator items (with a column indicating ownership), or should there be separate registries?

  4. Platform copies. .claude/skills/ and .claude/agents/ are generated from .lore/. Should operator-owned items also get platform copies, or only system items?

  5. Validation scope. Should validate-consistency.sh check operator-owned skills for frontmatter compliance, or only system items?

Decisions

1. Ownership: lore- prefix only, no frontmatter ownership field

The lore- prefix already naturally separates framework commands from everything else. No additional metadata needed.

Ownership rules:

  • lore-* skills and agents = framework-owned, overwritten on sync
  • Non-lore-* skills and agents = operator-owned from birth, never touched by sync
  • Tier 2 (discovered gotchas) = operator-owned immediately — Lore creates the file, operator owns it

Drop scope: internal — dead metadata, nothing reads it. Prefix naming is sufficient.

No owner frontmatter field — redundant with prefix convention. One source of truth, not two.

2. Agent generation scoped to framework skills

generate-agents.sh only auto-generates agents for domains that come from lore-* skills. If an operator adds a skill with domain: Infrastructure, Lore does not auto-create an infrastructure agent — the operator creates one explicitly if they want one.

3. Agent naming: lore- prefix for framework agents

Framework agents follow the same lore-* convention as skills. documentation-agent → lore-documentation-agent. Only 1 agent today — trivial migration.

4. Registry columns: no ownership column

The lore- prefix is self-documenting in the registry table. Adding a derived column is redundant.

5. Platform copies: copy ALL skills and agents

sync-platform-skills.sh copies all skills and agents to platform directories (.claude/skills/, .claude/agents/, etc.), regardless of ownership. The ownership boundary controls what sync overwrites, not what platforms can see. Operator skills need platform copies to function.

6. Validation: all skills, stricter for framework

validate-consistency.sh checks all skills for basic frontmatter (name, description, domain). Broken frontmatter breaks registries regardless of ownership. Only lore-* skills get stricter checks (user-invocable required, allowed-tools required).

7. Rediscovery: warn and skip

If a skill directory already exists when the agent tries to create it, surface a message (“skill already exists, review and update manually if needed”). Don’t auto-update — operator owns it. The agent can read the existing skill and suggest changes, but the operator decides.

8. Model preferences: per-platform fields, no translation layer

Skills are knowledge documents — model doesn’t apply to them. Model lives on agents only (execution contexts).

Agent frontmatter uses explicit per-platform fields:

---
name: lore-consolidation-agent
domain: Orchestrator
claude-model: opus
opencode-model: openai/o3
cursor-model: # not yet supported
skills:
  - lore-consolidate
---

Each platform reads only its own field. No automatic translation — the operator sets exactly what they want per platform. This supports mixed-provider setups (e.g., Claude models on Claude Code, OpenAI models on OpenCode).

Instance defaults in .lore-config under subagentDefaults:

{
  "version": "0.7.0",
  "subagentDefaults": {
    "claude-model": "sonnet",
    "opencode-model": "openai/gpt-4o",
    "cursor-model": null
  }
}

The parent key subagentDefaults makes it clear these are defaults for spawned subagents, not the orchestrator (which uses whatever model the session is running).

Resolution cascade:

  1. Agent frontmatter claude-model / opencode-model / cursor-model — wins if set
  2. .lore-config subagentDefaults — instance default
  3. Platform session model — fallback if nothing configured

sync-platform-skills.sh resolves the cascade at copy time: reads .lore-config defaults, checks agent frontmatter for overrides, writes the resolved value as the platform’s native model field in the generated copy.

No model_fast field. There’s no runtime mechanism that auto-selects between tiers. The calling agent chooses the model per-invocation via the Task tool’s model parameter. One model per platform per agent is sufficient.

Implementation Scope

When ready to implement:

  • sync-framework.sh — filter skill/agent sync loop to lore-* only
  • generate-agents.sh — filter to lore-* skill domains only
  • sync-platform-skills.sh — resolve model cascade (agent frontmatter → .lore-config subagentDefaults → omit), write platform-native model field in generated copies
  • lib/config.js — read subagentDefaults from .lore-config
  • Rename documentation-agent → lore-documentation-agent
  • Drop scope: internal from all skill frontmatter
  • Update lore-create-skill flow — no scope field in template
  • Update lore-create-agent flow — include platform model fields, lore- prefix for framework agents
  • Update validate-consistency.sh — tiered validation (basic for all, strict for lore-*)
  • Update lore-docs configuration guide with subagentDefaults and model fields
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
